name: CuPy
summary: Drop-in GPU replacement for NumPy using CUDA. Same API, all operations execute on NVIDIA GPU memory. Use when NumPy is the bottleneck and you have VRAM headroom.

tasks.task: Move data between CPU and GPU
tasks.mental_trigger: I have a NumPy array and need to run operations on GPU, or I need to bring GPU results back to CPU
tasks.syntax:
cp.array(numpy_arr)
cp.asarray(numpy_arr)
numpy_arr = cp.asnumpy(gpu_arr)
numpy_arr = gpu_arr.get()
important_params: dtype — specify to avoid unexpected upcasting during transfer
stream — CuPy stream for async transfer overlap with compute
tasks.example:
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp

x_cpu = np.random.rand(10000, 512).astype(np.float32)
x_gpu = cp.asarray(x_cpu)          # CPU → GPU

result_gpu = cp.dot(x_gpu, x_gpu.T)
result_cpu = result_gpu.get()       # GPU → CPU
tasks.use_when: You need the GPU for a specific bottleneck operation (matmul, SVD, FFT) inside a larger CPU pipeline. Transfer once, compute many times.
tasks.avoid_when: The data is small — PCIe transfer overhead dominates for arrays under ~10MB. Use NumPy directly instead.
tasks.decision_notes: Transfer cost is O(n) on PCIe bandwidth (~16 GB/s). For a 100MB array that means ~6ms transfer vs nanoseconds for an in-memory NumPy copy. Batch all GPU work to amortize this cost.
gotchas: cp.array() is synchronous and blocks the host until the copy completes — use streams for overlap with computation.
cp.empty() allocates uninitialized GPU memory; previous allocations may leave garbage values — always initialize before reading.

tasks.task: Accelerate math and linear algebra on GPU
tasks.mental_trigger: I need matrix multiply, SVD, FFT, or reduction operations to run faster than NumPy on CPU
tasks.syntax:
cp.dot(a, b)
a @ b
cp.linalg.solve(A, b)
cp.linalg.svd(A, full_matrices=False)
cp.sum(a, axis)
cp.mean(a, axis)
important_params: full_matrices=False — economy SVD; avoids allocating O(m*n) for large matrices
axis — 0 for column-wise, 1 for row-wise reduction
dtype — float32 is 2x faster than float64 on most CUDA hardware
tasks.example:
# Batched matrix multiply on GPU
A = cp.random.rand(1000, 512).astype(cp.float32)
B = cp.random.rand(512, 256).astype(cp.float32)
C = A @ B                                # GPU matmul

# Economy SVD
U, s, Vh = cp.linalg.svd(A, full_matrices=False)

# Scalar result needs .item() for Python float
mean_val = cp.mean(A).item()
tasks.use_when: Matrix operations on arrays larger than ~1000x1000. FFT on large 1D/2D signals. Any reduction where NumPy is the observed bottleneck in a profiler.
tasks.avoid_when: Operations with irregular memory access patterns (e.g. Python loops over elements) — GPU parallelism cannot be exploited and cupy overhead makes it slower than NumPy.
tasks.decision_notes: Use float32 instead of float64 unless you have a precision requirement. GPU throughput for float32 is 2-32x higher depending on the NVIDIA architecture (Ampere+).
gotchas: Reduction ops like cp.sum() return a 0-d CuPy array — call .item() to get a Python scalar; otherwise comparisons with Python floats silently wrap in unexpected ways.
Large SVD allocates all three matrices (U, S, Vh) simultaneously on GPU — use full_matrices=False to avoid OOM on tall/wide matrices.

tasks.mental_trigger: I need to compute Fourier transforms on large signals and NumPy FFT is too slow
tasks.syntax:
cp.fft.fft(a, n)
cp.fft.ifft(a)
cp.fft.fft2(a, s)
cp.fft.fftshift(x)
important_params: n — output length; pads or truncates input to this size
axis — axis along which to compute; default is last axis
tasks.example:
signal = cp.random.rand(1024 * 1024).astype(cp.float32)
spectrum = cp.fft.fft(signal)
magnitude = cp.abs(spectrum)
shifted = cp.fft.fftshift(magnitude)
tasks.use_when: Signal processing, audio feature extraction, or image frequency analysis where batch FFT over thousands of signals is required.
tasks.avoid_when: Short signals (< 1024 samples) — overhead of GPU kernel launch exceeds compute savings. Use scipy.fft on CPU for short arrays.
tasks.decision_notes: CuPy FFT delegates to cuFFT under the hood, which achieves near-theoretical peak throughput on NVIDIA GPUs. For batch 1D FFT over thousands of signals, use cp.fft.fft on a 2D array (one signal per row).
gotchas: cp.fft.fft operates on the last axis by default — for 2D arrays, this may not be the axis you intend. Always specify axis explicitly.
Passing n changes frequency resolution; this is silent and can corrupt downstream frequency-domain analysis if you miscount bins.
